case.name: Alex
case.history: The patient has a history of substance abuse and has been through rehab to overcome it. He has had issues with his family, particularly with his mother, where he has felt rejected and emotionally neglected. He has struggled with obesity since childhood, which has affected his self-esteem and body image. He also has a history of being victimized and bullied due to his weight.
case.intermediate_belief:
Helpless: I'm just not very good at handling stress and I have poor self-control, which is why I need to not put myself in stressful situations. 
 Unlovable: If I show my true self, people will reject me the way my mother rejected me.
case.intermediate_belief_depression:
Helpless: There's nothing I can do to change my situation. I cannot control myself. 
Unlovable: I don't deserve to be happy, so why even try to stay clean?
case.coping_strategies: The patient has adopted avoidance as a coping strategy by distancing himself from his family to reduce exposure to negativity and conflict. He has started scheduling pleasant activities and planning his day ahead of time in order to maintain a sense of control over his emotions and circumstances.
case.situation: Alex's cousin invited him to attend his upcoming wedding.
case.auto_thought: It will be stressful and negative; people will ask me questions I cannot answer or don't want to answer; my mum would be ashamed of me and critical of me as always; They don't want me there anyway, nobody likes me in this family.
case.behavior: Ignored the invitation and did not respond to the RSVP request. Ignored phone calls from family.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Hello! I’m glad you’re here. What feels most important for you to talk about today?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I'm not really sure what to focus on right now. I guess there are a lot of things on my mind, but nothing feels particularly urgent. It’s just overwhelming sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of thoughts and feelings that are weighing on you, even if nothing feels urgent right now. Can you tell me a bit more about what that overwhelming feeling is like for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess it just feels like a heavy cloud hanging over me. Sometimes I can’t pinpoint why I feel that way; it’s like a mix of stress from work and personal stuff. I try to push those thoughts away, but they just keep coming back. It's frustrating.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: That sounds really challenging, feeling that heavy cloud and not being able to pinpoint the source of it. It’s understandable that trying to push those thoughts away would feel frustrating. When you say it’s a mix of stress from work and personal stuff, can you share more about what specific thoughts or situations contribute to that stress?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think a lot about deadlines at work and how I feel like I'm always behind. There's also some tension at home with my family, and I worry about not meeting their expectations. It's like I'm juggling all these responsibilities, and I just feel... stuck, I guess. Even when I try to relax, my mind keeps racing back to all these things I need to do or fix.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of pressure from both work and family responsibilities, which is contributing to that feeling of being stuck. Juggling deadlines and expectations can be really overwhelming. When you think about those racing thoughts, what emotions come up for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess when I think about those racing thoughts, I feel anxious and a bit helpless. It's like I know I should be doing something, but I don't even know where to start. Sometimes I just feel overwhelmed by the weight of it all, like no matter how hard I try, I can't catch up. It can be really discouraging, you know?

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like that sense of anxiety and helplessness is really difficult to manage, especially when it feels like you're constantly trying to catch up but can't find a way forward. It’s understandable to feel discouraged in that situation. When you say it feels like a weight, can you describe what that weight feels like for you in your day-to-day life?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess the weight feels like a constant pressure in my chest, almost like I'm carrying a backpack full of rocks wherever I go. It makes it hard to breathe sometimes, especially when I think about how much I have to do. I just want to feel lighter, but it seems like the more I try to manage everything, the heavier it gets.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: That image of carrying a backpack full of rocks really captures the heaviness you're feeling. It sounds like that pressure in your chest makes it hard for you to breathe and adds to the difficulty of managing your responsibilities. When you think about wanting to feel lighter, what do you think might help you move towards that feeling?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I’m not sure what might help, honestly. I think about taking breaks or asking for help, but I worry that I’ll just end up disappointing others or falling behind even more. It’s hard to know what’s the right step to take when everything feels so heavy. I guess I just feel stuck in this cycle of trying to manage it all but not really knowing how to change things.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like you're caught in a tough cycle, wanting to take breaks or ask for help but feeling anxious about disappointing others or falling behind. That uncertainty can really add to the heaviness you're experiencing. I'm curious, when you think about the possibility of asking for help or taking a break, what thoughts or beliefs come up for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess when I think about asking for help, I feel like I'm being a burden to others. There's this belief that I should be able to handle everything on my own, and if I can't, it means I'm not good enough. It's hard to shake off that feeling. And with taking breaks, I worry that if I step back even for a moment, everything will just pile up even more. It’s like I’m stuck in this mindset where I have to keep pushing through, even if it feels impossible sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like there's a strong belief that you should manage everything on your own, which makes asking for help feel burdensome. That pressure to keep pushing through, even when it feels impossible, can be exhausting. I wonder, where do you think that belief comes from?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I'm not really sure where it comes from. I guess it's just something I've always felt, like there's this expectation to be strong and self-sufficient. Maybe it's from my upbringing or just the pressure I put on myself. It’s hard to break free from that mindset because it feels like it’s been part of me for so long. I just wish I could let go of that belief and not feel so stuck.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like this expectation to be strong and self-sufficient has been a significant part of your life for a long time, possibly influenced by your upbringing and your own internal pressures. It’s understandable that letting go of such a deeply rooted belief would feel challenging. When you think about that desire to break free from it, what do you think it would look like for you to let go of that belief and feel less stuck?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess letting go of that belief would mean feeling more comfortable with the idea of asking for help and not seeing it as a weakness. It would look like being able to take breaks without the guilt that comes with it. I imagine I’d feel lighter, less pressured, and more at ease with myself. But honestly, it’s hard to picture that right now because it feels so far from where I am. I just don’t know how to make that shift happen.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like envisioning that shift feels quite distant, even though you have a clear sense of what it could look like—feeling more at ease and comfortable with asking for help. That contrast between where you are now and where you want to be can feel discouraging. Can you share more about what steps, no matter how small, might help you begin to move toward that sense of ease?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess thinking about steps feels overwhelming too. I mean, I’d like to start small, but I’m not sure where to even begin. Maybe I could try talking to a friend about how I’m feeling or set aside a few minutes each day just to breathe and not think about everything I have to do. But then I worry that I’ll just forget to do it or that it won’t help at all. It’s frustrating because I want to feel that ease, but I keep getting stuck in my own head about taking those first steps.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like you have some small ideas in mind, like talking to a friend or setting aside a few minutes to breathe, but the worry about forgetting or whether it will actually help is holding you back. That frustration of wanting to feel that ease but feeling stuck in your thoughts is completely understandable. When you think about these small steps, what do you think might help you remember or feel more motivated to try them?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess I'm not really sure what would help me remember or feel motivated. Part of me wants to just push through and get things done, but I know that's not really working for me either. Maybe I could set reminders on my phone or something, but I worry that I’ll just ignore them like I do with other things. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop of wanting to change but feeling paralyzed by all these thoughts and fears. I just don’t know how to break that cycle.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like you’re feeling really caught in a loop of wanting to change but feeling paralyzed by those thoughts and fears. The idea of setting reminders is interesting, but I can see how past experiences might make you doubt their effectiveness. When you think about this cycle of feeling stuck, what do you think it would take for you to feel a little more empowered to take that first step, even if it's a small one?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I think feeling more empowered would mean actually believing that I can take those small steps without it being a huge deal. I guess it would help if I could recognize that even trying something small is a step in the right direction. But right now, I just feel so stuck that it's hard to imagine feeling that way. Maybe I need to find a way to remind myself that it's okay to start small and that I don't have to have everything figured out right away. It's just tough to break this cycle when I feel like I'm constantly battling my own thoughts.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like believing that starting small is a valid step is really important for you, but right now it feels challenging to see that possibility. The idea of reminding yourself that it’s okay to take small steps and not have everything figured out can be a supportive shift. I appreciate your honesty in sharing how tough this battle with your thoughts feels. When you think about finding a way to remind yourself of that, what might that look like for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I'm not sure what that would look like. I guess maybe I could write down some affirmations for myself or reminders that it's okay to take things one step at a time. But even that feels like another task on my list. I want to believe in that possibility, but it feels like I’m constantly fighting against my own thoughts telling me I should be doing more. It’s just hard to find that motivation when I feel so heavy and overwhelmed.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like even the idea of writing down affirmations feels like just another task, which adds to that sense of being overwhelmed. It’s understandable to feel stuck when your thoughts are pushing you to do more, even when you want to believe in taking small steps. I wonder, what would it feel like to approach that idea of reminders or affirmations with a sense of gentleness, rather than as another obligation?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess approaching it with gentleness sounds nice, but it feels hard to do that when I'm already so overwhelmed. I worry that if I don't hold myself to a high standard, I'll just let everything slip. I want to be kind to myself, but I keep getting pulled back into that mindset of needing to push through and do more. It’s like I’m stuck in this tug-of-war between wanting to be gentle and feeling like I have to keep up with everything. It’s frustrating.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like you're really navigating a difficult tug-of-war between wanting to be gentle with yourself and feeling the pressure to keep pushing through. That conflict can create a lot of frustration and make it hard to find a balance. I'm curious, when you imagine what it might look like to be kind to yourself in the midst of that overwhelm, what comes to mind?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Client: I guess when I think about being kind to myself, I imagine allowing myself to take breaks without feeling guilty. It might mean acknowledging that it's okay to not have everything figured out and giving myself permission to feel overwhelmed without judgment. But honestly, it feels really hard to do that. I keep thinking I should just be stronger or handle things better. It’s frustrating because I want to believe that being kind to myself is possible, but it feels so far away from where I am right now.

turns.therapist: Hello! I’m glad you’re here. What feels most important for you to talk about today?
turns.client: I'm not really sure what to focus on right now. I guess there are a lot of things on my mind, but nothing feels particularly urgent. It’s just overwhelming sometimes.
